text: The V-Model is a software development methodology where each development phase has a corresponding testing phase. The left side represents development phases (requirements, design, implementation) and the right side represents testing phases (acceptance, integration, unit testing). The shape forms a V — development goes down, testing goes up, meeting at implementation.

V-Model phase mapping: Business Requirements → User Acceptance Testing (UAT). System Design → System Testing. Architecture Design → Integration Testing. Module Design → Unit Testing. Implementation sits at the bottom connecting both sides. Each testing phase validates the corresponding development phase — not just the code.

V-Model advantage is that testing is planned early — test cases are written during the corresponding design phase, not after development. This catches requirement ambiguities before code is written. Defects found early are exponentially cheaper to fix than defects found in production.

V-Model limitation is that it assumes requirements are stable and well-defined upfront. It is less suitable for projects where requirements evolve frequently. Modern teams adapt V-Model principles within agile — writing test cases during sprint planning, not after development.

Verification vs Validation in V-Model: Verification asks 'are we building the product right?' — checking processes, documents, and design against requirements. Validation asks 'are we building the right product?' — checking the actual working software against user needs. Both are necessary — a perfectly built wrong product still fails.
